title: What can financial regulators learn from Formula 1?
gist: Using the FIA's regulation of Formula 1 as a model, the author argues financial regulators should abandon the default assumption that innovation is broadly beneficial, instead proactively blocking innovation with unproven societal value (e.g. stablecoins) while directing resources toward areas like cybersecurity, and adopting faster, harder-to-appeal enforcement.

insights.claim: The FIA regulates nearly every aspect of Formula 1 simultaneously — technical car standards, race conduct, team spending caps and even driver media obligations — to serve multiple goals at once: safety, commercial spectacle, and technical innovation with automotive-sector spillovers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Offers a template for regulators who must balance stability, innovation and growth simultaneously rather than treating them as separate silos.

insights.claim: Formula 1's commercial rights are valued at $23 billion, the British Grand Prix at Silverstone draws roughly half a million spectators and 80 million TV viewers, and the F1 movie became the most successful sports film ever made — evidence the sport's regulatory model coexists with, and arguably enables, enormous commercial success.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Counters the assumption that heavy, detailed regulation is inherently a drag on commercial growth.
insights.locator: This weekend's British Grand Prix at Silverstone

insights.claim: F1 cars are deliberately not engineered to be the fastest possible; electronic driver aids were banned in 1994 both for safety and to preserve the role of driver skill, showing regulation is used to shape competitive dynamics, not just to cap risk.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Illustrates that regulators can deliberately suppress certain technologies to preserve a system's desired character, a mindset largely absent from financial regulation.
insights.locator: they are not designed to be the fastest possible

insights.claim: Only one F1 driver has died in a race since 2000, compared to 12 deaths between 1970 and 1999, a reduction the author attributes to highly detailed regulation combined with mandated safety innovation such as the titanium 'halo' cockpit protection required from 2018.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that prescriptive, mandated technical standards can materially cut tail-risk outcomes, a case study regulators in other domains could cite when pushing for mandatory safety tech.
insights.locator: 12 drivers died during races from 1970 to 1999

insights.claim: Unlike the FIA's approach to driver safety, financial regulators typically do not try to eliminate all risk, reasoning that the level of regulation required would prevent the financial system from performing its core functions — but the author argues F1's experience suggests this assumption should be tested.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Challenges a foundational assumption of financial regulatory design; worth watching whether regulators pick up more F1-style prescriptive risk elimination.
insights.locator: Financial regulators seek to prevent losses to retail consumers

insights.claim: Digital communication and AI could let regulators collect and process far higher volumes of data on financial institutions' activity at minimal cost to the institutions themselves, improving detection of illegal practice or excessive risk-taking without necessarily reducing the system's value to society.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Points to AI-driven supervisory technology (suptech) as a growth area regulators may expand into, relevant for compliance-tech vendors and regulated institutions alike.
insights.locator: digital communication and artificial intelligence could allow regulators

insights.claim: The FIA does not assume all innovation benefits F1; it uses detailed regulation to deliberately block innovation in some areas and drive it in others, focusing constructor resources on what it judges most valuable — such as driver safety or sustainable fuels.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The central mental model of the piece: regulation as an active steering mechanism for R&D allocation, not just a risk floor.
insights.locator: The second insight is on innovation

insights.claim: By contrast, financial regulators default to assuming innovation is broadly beneficial and only move to limit it once they see evidence of substantial risk; the author argues regulators should instead be willing to block innovation such as stablecoins where societal benefit is hard to demonstrate, while proactively directing resources toward areas like cybersecurity where benefits are clear.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A direct, actionable regulatory-stance call: expect friction for stablecoin issuers/adopters if regulators adopt this FIA-inspired posture, and expect regulatory tailwinds for cybersecurity-focused fintech infrastructure.
insights.quote: A key lesson from F1 could be that regulators should be more willing to block innovation by financial institutions in some areas, such as stablecoins, where the benefits to society are hard to demonstrate, and instead focus it even more proactively on areas like cybersecurity
insights.locator: By contrast financial regulators tend to start from the assumption

insights.claim: The 2026 F1 technical overhaul eliminated complex energy-recovery and drag-reduction overtaking aids, replacing them with a larger electric motor and smaller combustion engine (roughly 50:50 hybrid split) and a new mandate for 100% sustainable fuel, redirecting team R&D toward hybrid powertrains and sustainable fuel technology.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Real example of a regulator using rule design to force an entire industry's R&D spend toward a specific technology goal (sustainable fuel) rather than leaving it to market choice.
insights.locator: The 2026 regulatory update was one of the most far reaching

insights.claim: Major car manufacturers including Mercedes, VW and Renault own F1 teams specifically because the sport's intensely competitive, regulation-directed innovation environment is valuable to them.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Suggests well-designed, directive regulation can attract capital and corporate participation rather than repel it — a counter to the common view that heavier regulation deters investment.
insights.locator: The intensely competitive innovative environment in F1

insights.claim: A regulator inspired by the FIA's approach would find it easier to decide on addressing perverse incentives through bonus caps, using regulation to force the financial system to support the net-zero transition, and applying size caps on financial institutions to preserve competition.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Flags several concrete regulatory tools (bonus caps, net-zero mandates, size caps) that could see renewed momentum if this directive-regulation philosophy gains traction among policymakers.
insights.locator: More broadly, a regulator inspired by the FIA's approach

insights.claim: F1 regulations are developed in close consultation with teams but then rigorously enforced, with some penalties applied in real time during races and frequent, fast-moving inquiries into technical breaches that can result in demotion or disqualification; teams do not hesitate to report each other.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Suggests financial regulators could improve deterrence by imposing more varied prudential penalties more swiftly and with fewer grounds for appeal, echoing F1's enforcement speed.
insights.locator: Regulations are developed through close consultation with F1 teams
